coredata.dc:title: A new paradigm in biosensing: MOF-carbon dot conjugates
coredata.dc:creator: Nannuri, Shivanand H.
Pandey, Abhijeet
Kulkarni, Sanjay
Deshmukh, Prashant K.
George, Sajan D.
Mutalik, Srinivas
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as promising nanocarriers for targeted drug delivery, owing to their unique structural properties and tunable pore sizes. Integrating carbon dots (C-dots) into MOFs will further enhance their functionality and imaging capabilities. In this study, the synthesis and characterization of C-dots loaded MOF is detailed as a novel pH and temperature-responsive nanoplatform for the imaging and therapy of neuroblastoma. The C-dots were synthesized using a modified microwave method, and their structural and optical properties were characterized using techniques such as XRD, FTIR, and XPS. The C-dots demonstrated a hexagonal graphite structure with hydroxyl and carboxyl surface functional groups. The C-dots also exhibited green emission under excitation at 405 nm with concentration-dependent emission behaviour. The pH-dependent emission studies reveal a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity from pH 3–9, followed by a rapid decrease at higher pH values. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased by ∼50% upon increasing the temperature (30–65 °C) at a step of 5 °C covering the range of physiological temperature. The photothermal studies elucidated that the C-dots exhibit good photothermal conversion properties upon NIR laser irradiation that can find applications in the areas like photothermal therapy. The drug release studies confirmed the pH-responsive drug release property of the nanocomposite. Further, the surface-modified nanocomposite was efficiently uptaken by the neuroblastoma cells suggesting the possibility of targeted therapy for cancer. Overall, this study gives new insights into the synthesis, characterisation, and functionalization of C-dots loaded with ZIF-8 NPs, revealing their potential for drug delivery applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Theragnosis
ZIF-8
C-dots
Stimuli-responsive
Hybrid-nanocomposite
Neuroblastoma

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) by microwave method
Cell viability and cellular uptake studies
Drug loading and drug release studies
Cell viability and cellular uptake studies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are also thankful to Manipal College of Pharmaceutical Sciences and Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, India, for providing the necessary facilities to carry out this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the procedure adopted for the synthesis of C-dots.
Spectral profiles of C-dots (A) XRD diffractogram (B) FT-IR spectrum.
XPS spectra of C-dots (A) survey scan, (B) C1 s spectrum, (C) O1 s spectrum, and (D) Na1 s spectrum.
(A) UV-Vis absorption, (B) Emission spectrum of the C-dots prepared by MW method at 405 nm. (C) Emission spectra (excitation wavelength-dependent) of C-dots.
(A) Concentration dependent emission spectra (B) zeta-potential plot of C-dots.
(A) pH-dependent emission spectra of C-dots (B) Linear relationship between the acidic pH values and C-dots’ fluorescence intensity. Inset: PL emission intensity dependance on different pH (pH 3–12).
(A) Temperature-dependent emission spectra and (B) Linear relationship between the temperature (30–65 °C) and C-dots’ fluorescence intensity.
(A) Experimental setup with 980 nm NIR laser for Photothermal studies. (B) Photothermal heating curves of C-dots at different concentrations and DI water under NIR laser irradiation.
Spectral Analysis of ZIF-8 @C-dot. (A) FTIR spectrum, (B) Raman spectrum and (C) XRD plot.
Surface morphology and EDS mapping of ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs. (A) SEM, (B) TEM and (C) EDS mapping.

Drug release studies and Stability studies of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot in different media. (A) In vitro drug release data, (B) Interaction with serum protein, (C) Interaction with Erythrocytes, (D) Cellular internalization of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot in IMR-32 cells.

head.ce:title: A new paradigm in biosensing: MOF-carbon dot conjugates

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dr. Rajendra Gode College of Pharmacy, Malkapur, Dist: Buldhana 443101, Maharashtra, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as promising nanocarriers for targeted drug delivery, owing to their unique structural properties and tunable pore sizes. Integrating carbon dots (C-dots) into MOFs will further enhance their functionality and imaging capabilities. In this study, the synthesis and characterization of C-dots loaded MOF is detailed as a novel pH and temperature-responsive nanoplatform for the imaging and therapy of neuroblastoma. The C-dots were synthesized using a modified microwave method, and their structural and optical properties were characterized using techniques such as XRD, FTIR, and XPS. The C-dots demonstrated a hexagonal graphite structure with hydroxyl and carboxyl surface functional groups. The C-dots also exhibited green emission under excitation at 405 nm with concentration-dependent emission behaviour. The pH-dependent emission studies reveal a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity from pH 3–9, followed by a rapid decrease at higher pH values. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased by ∼50% upon increasing the temperature (30–65 °C) at a step of 5 °C covering the range of physiological temperature. The photothermal studies elucidated that the C-dots exhibit good photothermal conversion properties upon NIR laser irradiation that can find applications in the areas like photothermal therapy. The drug release studies confirmed the pH-responsive drug release property of the nanocomposite. Further, the surface-modified nanocomposite was efficiently uptaken by the neuroblastoma cells suggesting the possibility of targeted therapy for cancer. Overall, this study gives new insights into the synthesis, characterisation, and functionalization of C-dots loaded with ZIF-8 NPs, revealing their potential for drug delivery applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The ultimate goal for any drug delivery system is to maximize therapeutic efficacy and minimize side effects. A lot of work has been put on this front to develop novel micro or nanoplatforms having novel features that enable them to release the drug molecule in a controlled manner to a specific target site. This effort has been fuelled by the exploding growth of materials chemistry. In order to provide structural control at the molecular level, Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are being explored widely. MOFs are composed of metal ions and organic ligands linked via coordinative bonding forming highly porous, three-dimensional network of crystalline structures. A range of MOFs with tailored physicochemical features (like surface area, shape, pore diameter and hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity) can be created for specific purposes by selecting appropriate linkers and metal ions [1]. Particularly, the adaptable properties of MOFs, such as their modifiable porosity, customizable size and structure along with ease of surface functionalization, have made them one of the most widely used materials in the biomedical field, where drug delivery holds great promise for future growth. Lead and other heavy metal contaminants or harmful chemicals in wastewater can also be detected and absorbed using porous materials [2,3]. Their large surface area, and other properties allow for the efficient detection and removal of such contaminants, providing possible solutions for environmental remediation and water quality monitoring. Amongst the several thousands of MOFs, widely utilized in Drug Delivery Systems (DDS) are zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs). They are a subfamily of Zn-MOFs coupled by Zn(II) and imidazolate. The most typical of them is ZIF-8 formed when Zn2+ and the Nitrogen atom coordinate on the 2-methylimidazole ring. ZIF-8 performs exceptionally well in controlled drug delivery because it is acid-sensitive and thus, possesses a pH-responsive drug delivery application [4].

In the realm of bio-imaging or bio-sensing, C-dots have gained considerable attention as fluorescent nanomaterials [5]. They have remarkable nanostructures with promising optical, thermal, electronic, chemical inertness and biocompatible properties [6,7]. Since carbon is an essential component of organic matter, the formation of C-dots uses a variety of naturally occurring precursors. C-dots have a particle size < 10 nm and its quantum confinement produce emission qualities that depend on particle size. Advanced approaches are being proposed on incorporating the dual emissive properties to C-dots and demonstrating their sensing applications as ratiometric nanoprobes [8]. Furthermore, the surface states play an important role in determining the C-dots' emission characteristics. Despite the fact that the precise mechanism causing C-dot emission is yet unknown, it may be caused by the carbon core of conjugated systems, surface states associated with functional groups or molecular states brought on by the bound fluorescent molecules. Researchers are investigating numerous techniques to create C-dots having customizable C-dot features including size, shape, and carbonization degree because the preparation method plays a significant part in the process [9].

Our group is extensively working on the preparation and surface modification of nanoparticles and MOFs for targeted delivery of the anticancer molecules to brain tumours like glioblastoma and neuroblastoma [10–14]. It is evident from our earlier reports that ZIF-8 functions as a pH-responsive drug delivery system and can entrap up to 50% of the drug molecules. In continuation to this, we are exploring the theranostic application of ZIF-8 in this article by entrapping C-dots functioning as a pH and temperature responsive imaging agent and anticancer drug 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) as a therapeutic agent. The first section describes about the preparation and extensive characterization of the C-dots followed by the entrapment of C-dots within the framework of ZIF-8. 5-FU loading imparts therapeutic ability to the MOF. The MOF was further surface functionalized with Hyaluronic acid (HA), a negatively charged natural polysaccharide having good biocompatibility [1]. Additionally, HA have high affinity to the CD44 receptors overexpressed on the tumour cell surface, which allows it to actively target specific tumour cells. This receptor-based interaction enables the phagocytosis of the MOF into the cancer cell and hyaluronidase present in microenvironment of tumour, can break down the HA outer layer exposing the nanoplatform to the acidic tumour environment [15].

The findings of this work will be important in understanding the construction of multifunctional nanocarriers for the targeted delivery of therapeutic agents. The combination of ZIF-8, C-dots, and HA will provide a synergistic strategy in integrating the imaging capabilities, controlled drug release, and targeted delivery approaches, to improve the therapeutic effects of cancer treatment. Comprehensive characterization and evaluation of these nanocomposites will provide vital insights into their potential as efficient and tailored drug delivery carriers, with the ultimate goal of enhancing cancer therapy outcomes.

2-Methyl imidazole (hereafter mentioned as linker), Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (hereafter mentioned as Zn salt) were purchased from Himedia. Hyaluronic acid was gifted from Novozymes Denmark. 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and glucose were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Bangalore, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) by microwave method
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesized based on the literature with some modification via microwave method by using CEM Discover SP Microwave synthesizer [5]. Here, glucose was used as a carbon source in water as shown in the schematic Fig. 1. In a typical procedure, 1.5 g glucose and 0.2 g NaOH were added in 50 mL water under stirring. The solution was then transferred into a microwave vial and heated at 160 °C for 10 min at 300 W power. The carbon dots solution purified from unreacted organic impurities by washing with dichloromethane. For further purification carbon dots solution dialyzed using dialysis bag (MWCO≈2000 Da) for 24 h and then dried at 80 °C to obtain the dried C-Dots (black powder).

C-dots loaded ZIF-8 (ZIF-8 @C-dots) NPs was prepared using one-pot method according to the method reported previously [12,13]. Briefly, Zn salt (20 mM) was dissolved in 10 mL water and diluted C-dot solution was added to it. The above solution mixture was added to the linker solution (80 mM) prepared in 10 mL and stirred for 10 min. The formation of ZIF-8 @C-dots was confirmed by the conversion of clear solution to milky dispersion. The dispersion was centrifuged to obtain the product, was washed thrice with water to remove unreacted chemicals and dried at 60 °C overnight.

5-FU drug was used in the drug loading experiments to estimate the drug loading efficiency of the ZIF-8 @C-dots. The drug loading studies was done using solvent impregnation technique by incubating the 5-FU (10 mg/mL) in the dispersion of ZIF-8 @C-dots for 24 h. The entrapment efficiency was found by indirect method and the absorbance of obtained supernatant was analysed using UV-Vis spectroscopy [13].

The principle of carbodiimide chemistry was employed to modify the surface of ZIF-8 @C-dots with hyaluronic acid (HA@ZIF-8 @C-dots). Briefly, to a solution of calculated quantity of NHS and carbodiimide salt (EDC) in water, ZIF-8 @C-dots were added and dispersed. HA solution was then added to it and stirred overnight at 50 °C to produce HA@ZIF-8 @C-dots [10].

Particle size distribution and zeta potential of the formulations was analysed using Zeta sizer (Malvern). The formation of network was confirmed using different spectroscopic techniques. For FTIR analysis, ATR-FTIR instrument (Bruker Alpha II) was used, PXRD diffractogram was evaluated using X-ray Diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan), Raman spectroscopy was performed using Raman spectrometer (i-Raman Plus, USA). The surface morphology was examined using both Scanning (SEM) and Transmission (TEM) Electron microscopy.

Dialysis bag approach was adopted to perform the drug release experiment [16]. Overnight soaked dialysis bag was filled with the drug loaded ZIF-8, fastened at both the ends and dispersed in the receptor compartment of PBS buffer (pH 7.4 and 5.5). Temperature in the receptor compartment was maintained at 37 °C and aliquots were taken at fixed time points. The released drug was quantified by finding the absorbance of the aliquots using UV-Vis spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and cellular uptake studies
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability and cellular internalization of HA modified ZIF-8 confined C-Dots was assessed in Neuroblastoma cells i.e IMR-32 as per previously reported method by our group. Various concentration of nanoparticles (5–25 µg/mL) was incubated with IMR-32 cells once they were confluent. MTT assay was used to determine the effect of synthesized nanoparticles on IMR-32 cells.

Similarly, for cellular uptake studies, the synthesized nanoparticles were tagged with FITC (Fluorescein isothiocyanate). The sub-confluent IMR-32 cells were grown on coverslip and incubated with FITC tagged nanoparticles for 24 h. Post incubation, the cells were fixed and visualized under confocal microscope (Zeiss LSM 510-Germany) was used at 60 × to assess the uptake at excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of 488 nm and 560 nm respectively.

The synthesized MOFs were evaluated for stability in different pH and in biological conditions. pH plays an essential role in defining the stability of nano-formulations. The impact of pH on stability of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dots was assessed by dispersing surface modified formulation (5 mg) in two different pH buffers (pH: 5.5 and 7.4) overnight and then assessed for size using Zeta sizer. To understand the interaction between serum protein and HA@ZIF-8 @C-dots, 5 mg of MOF was dispersed in BSA of two different concentrations (2% and 4% w/v). The mixture was stirred at 37 °C and 100 rpm for 4 h and later, the mixture was then centrifuged to remove un-adsorbed BSA and the pellet was re-dispersed in water to analyse the change in charge and size of MOF. Further, the stability of MOFs was also evaluated by incubating the MOFs overnight at 37 ºC in the cell culture media (DMEM) supplemented with FBS (10%) and antibiotics.

The hemocompatibility of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dots was assessed by examining its interaction with erythrocytes using a previously reported method [12]. Briefly, blood was collected in the tube containing EDTA solution by the retro orbital method. Erythrocytes were separated by centrifugation and washed twice with saline. HA@ZIF-8 @C-dots was dispersed in PBS (5 mg/mL) followed by the incubation of the suspension at 37 °C for 2 h. Following incubation, the sample was microscopically examined using Motic microscope to observe for changes in shape of RBCs. The control was RBCs incubated with saline.

Hemocompatibility assay was evaluated by diluting the separated erythrocytes with saline in 1:1 ratio [17,18]. The obtained suspension was mixed with MOF suspension (5 mg/mL in PBS) in 1:4 ratio and was incubated for 60 min at 37 °C. Subsequently the intact erythrocytes were separated by centrifuging the incubated sample. The hemoglobin present in the obtained supernatant was allowed to undergo oxidation by keeping the centrifuge tubes open for 5 min followed by the measurement of its optical density at 545 nm.

The XRD diffractogram of the C-dots was recorded using the mentioned commercial powder XRD system and the obtained result is shown in Fig. 2A. The C-dots exhibit broad diffraction peaks centered at 2θ = 37.5° and 22.5°, which can be attributed to the high degree of (100) and (002) planes of disordered carbon atoms representing hexagonal graphite structure. By using Bragg's equation (d= nλ/2 sinθ) the d-spacing is calculated and the values are found to be approximately 3.94 Å and 2.39 Å for the (002) and (100) peaks, respectively. Here, n is a positive integer (1), λ is the X-ray wavelength used (CuKα, λ = 1.54 Å) and θ is the position of the diffraction peak [19]. The surface functional groups of the C-dots are investigated by using FTIR spectroscopy and FTIR spectra of the C-dot sample are shown in Fig. 2B. The FTIR spectra show the characteristic transmission bands of O–H (3366 cm−1), stretching vibration band of CO (1713 cm−1, 1604 cm−1), and the stretching vibration bands of C–O (1033 cm−1). The peaks at 2942 and 1378 cm−1 originate from the stretching and bending vibrations of C–H, CC, respectively [20].

The XPS measurement survey scan of C-dots presented in Fig. 3A depicts the existence of two major elements, carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s) at 284 eV and 531 eV respectively, and a trace amount of sodium (Na1s) at 1071 eV. The elemental composition of the C-dots accounts for 59.1%, 40.5%, and 0.4% of C, O, and Na elements, respectively. The C1s spectrum shown in Fig. 3B representing the carbon bonds; (C-O), (CO) and (C-C/CC), at binding energies 286.15 eV, 287.98 eV and 284.79 eV respectively. The O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 3C can be ascribed to the 530.86 eV (CO), 532.62 eV (C-O/C-O-C), and 535.51 eV (O-H). The Na1s spectrum as shown in Fig. 3D can be attributed to the Na1s at 1071.56 eV [21,22]. The presence of Na elements is due to the usage of NaOH in the C-dots synthesis and it is found in an almost negligible percentage. The results observed here from the FTIR and XPS spectra unambiguously confirm that the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface.

C-dots were characterized for optical properties by recording the absorption spectra and optical emission spectra. The corresponding spectra are presented in Fig. 4 A and 4B. Absorption spectra exhibit bands at 275 nm, representing the π-π * transitions of the conjugated CC bands [23]. Under 405 nm excitation, the carbon dots yield green emission at 520 nm, as shown in Fig. 4B. The inset photograph of the Fig. 4B illustrates the C-dots dispersed in water under visible light (left) and upon illumination at 405 nm (right). The emission spectra (excitation wavelength-dependent) of C-dots are presented in Fig. 4 C with 10 nm intervals from the range between 300 nm and 500 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 C with increasing excitation wavelength (300–370 nm), the emission spectra of C-dots exhibit an increase in emission with a broad emission peak around 450 nm and a shoulder peak at 525 nm. When the excitation wavelength is changed to 380 nm, the C-dots exhibit two well-resolved emission peaks at 450 nm and 525 nm. Further increase in the excitation wavelength (390–500 nm), the 525 nm emission became more prominent compared to the 450 nm emission peak and show a redshift in the emission peak from 525 nm to 575 nm. It is reported that the emission bands of C-dots at different wavelengths, originate from the different emission centers or different surface states owing to the existence of various functional groups on C-dots’ surface [24–26].

The concentration of C-dots was chosen in the range of 0.08–1 mg/mL. From the concentration-dependent emission shown in Fig. 5 A, the PL intensity is found to increase with increasing the concentration of C-dots and reaches a maximum value for 0.25 mg/mL C-dots. Further increases in C-dots concentration result in quenching of the PL intensity of C-dots due to self-absorption quenching and collisional quenching [27]. The measured zeta potential (shown in Fig. 5B) of the C-dots in DI water was − 52.5 mV which indicated its strong electrostatic stabilization in aqueous media.

Based on the literature, the media pH is expected to have a substantial influence on the C-dots emission intensity. Herein, pH of the solution was adjusted by using diluted nitric acid (HNO3) and ammonia (NH3). It is evident in Fig. 6A that the fluorescent intensity increases gradually while varying the pH values from 3–9, and the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly for the pH values ranging from 10 to 12. At higher pH values from 6 to 9, the emission peak is found to be slightly redshifted from 510 to 520 nm. The C-dots exhibit an excellent linearity between the optical emission intensity and the pH for the range from 4 to 8, with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9945 (Fig. 6B). Although various studies reported the pH-responsive emission properties for C-dots, still there is uncertainty regarding the mechanism involved in the pH-dependent emission. There are different mechanisms proposed to explain the pH-responsive emission properties for the C-dots including, protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface, aggregation of C-dots, change in the energy levels, pH-induced electron transfer. Among these proposed mechanisms, protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups on C-dots, is the widely accepted mechanisms, where the oxygen-containing groups could alter the energy levels of C-dots on protonation and deprotonation leading to the pH-responsive emission [28]. Yang et al. reported similar studies where fluorescence of the C-dots emission intensity is increased linearly between the range of 1.48–7.56 and a further increase in the pH value from 8 to 11 results in only a slight increment in the intensity of optical emission of the C-dots. Also, the redshift of fluorescence emission peak at high pH values is observed in the study. It can be ascribed to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups present on the C-dots’ surface at high pH values. The formation of a new surface state having a lower bandgap leads to the emission of fluorescence in higher wavelength [29]. Our results concur with this observation also can be attributed to the same origin of variation C-dot emission properties and shows the linear response towards the pH values in the range between 4 and 9. Therefore, these particles could be used as a potential pH sensor in acidic conditions.

The C-dots showed sensitive variation with respect to the temperature. Fig. 7 A shows the temperature-dependence emission spectra of the developed C-dots. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased by ∼50% upon increasing the temperature (30–65 °C) at a step of 5 °C covering the range of physiological temperature. Fig. 7B shows the excellent linear relationship between the optical emission intensity and the temperature with a correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.9955. Here, ‘I′ is the integrated intensity at various temperatures, Io is the integrated intensity at 30 °C. The temperature sensitivity is 0.87% K−1 obtained from the linear fit slope of Fig. 7B [30]. The temperature-dependent fluorescence intensity may be attributed to thermally activated nonradiative trapping. At lower temperature values, the nonradiative channels of surface states (trap/defect) are thermally activated and emit photons radiatively through the excited electrons. Further increase in the temperature, enhanced population of the non-radiative channels of surface states (trap/defect) and excited electrons returned to the ground state through non-radiative relaxation resulting in the quenching of C-dots emission [30]. Therefore, the C-dots have the potential to be developed as a sensitive temperature sensor.

In order to investigate C-dots for photothermal applications, 2 mL of C-dots solution was taken in a quartz cuvette and irradiated with a 980 nm CW laser at a 2 W power for 15 min. To record the photothermal performance of C-dots, a thermocouple probe was inserted into the cuvette, and the temperature change is measured in every 40 s. All the components of the measurement system were fixed onto an optical table to maintain stability and alignment, as shown in Fig. 8 A. The temperature rise or photothermal heating curves for C-dots solutions of various concentrations were shown in Fig. 8B. For the present studies, DI water was considered as the control sample for the temperature rise studies. Interestingly, the C-dots exhibited a strong capability in generating heat by absorbing the NIR light. As shown in Fig. 8B, by irradiating laser for 15 min, the C-dots solution with a concentration of 2.5, 5, 10 mg/mL exhibited a significant temperature rise of 16.9 °C, 17.5 °C, 19.3 °C, respectively, as compared to the initial temperature. In the initial 2 min of 980 nm laser irradiation at 2 W input power, the temperature rise for control (water alone) was measured to be 6 °C from the initial temperature (28 °C). On the other hand, 2.5, 5, and 10 mg/mL C-dots solution were shown to exhibit a rapid rise in the temperature around 8.3 °C, 8.8 °C, and 10.7 °C, respectively. Further laser irradiation for the next 12 min exhibited a slight increase in the temperature and reaching to a plateau region. It elucidates that the C-dots exhibit good photothermal conversion properties upon NIR laser irradiation that can find applications in the areas like photothermal therapy [31–33].

A simple one pot method was used in the preparation of ZIF-8 @C-dots. The linker reacts with the metal salt to form crystal structure of ZIF-8. Addition of C-dots together with the linker solution prior to the addition of the metal salt solution ensures the entrapment of C-dots within the framework of MOF. Obtained ZIF-8 @C-dots showed Particle size of 115 nm, PDI of 0.114 and a positive zeta potential of 28.1 mV.

The surface modification of ZIF-8 @C-dot was done with HA to impart CD44 receptor targeting ability to the nanoplatform. The principle of carbodiimide chemistry was employed for the conjugation of HA, wherein the coupling reagent (EDC/NHS) bring about crosslinking between HA and ZIF-8 [12]. The particle size of the HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot had slightly increased to 122 nm with a PDI of 0.182, owing to the coating of HA on the surface of ZIF-8. While the zetapotential value had dropped to 23.2 mV.

The FTIR spectrum ( Fig. 9A) of the ZIF-8 @C-dot showed the peaks at 2927.72 cm−1 and 3131.12 cm−1 representing the absorption of methyl group and stretching of imidazole ring respectively. The peak at 1576.85 correspond to CN stretching, and those at 1142.36 cm−1 and 993.91 cm−1 indicated the absorption and stretching vibrations of C-N respectively. These peaks together confirm the presence of imidazole in the ZIF-8 framework. The peaks at 3629.85 cm−1 and 1670.38 cm−1 correspond to the O-H stretching and CO stretching absorption peaks indicating the existence of C-dots [34]. The Raman spectra (Fig. 9B) of ZIF-8 @C-dot showed the bands at 2970.0 cm−1, 1543.2 cm−1, 1065.3 cm−1, 757.2 cm−1 and 273.1 cm−1 corresponding to the asymmetric stretching of C-H (methyl group), C4-C5 bond stretch, C5-N stretch, CN bending vibrations and Zn-N stretching vibrations respectively. These results were almost similar with those of the earlier reports [13]. XRD plot (Fig. 9C) of ZIF-8 @C-dot, displayed the diffraction peaks at 7.24°, 9.6°, 12.32°, 14.1°, and 17.36°. These peaks were similar to the earlier reports [35] and confirmed that the introduction of C-dots into the framework had no effect on the structure of ZIF-8 crystal.

The electron microscopic techniques (SEM; Fig. 10A and TEM; Fig. 10B) were used to assess the surface morphology of the ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs. The micrographs indicated that ZIF-8 NPs were spherical and had an average of 100 nm particle size. The surface modification smoothened the surface of ZIF-8. This smoothening of rough edges helps in minimizing the protein corona formation. Further, the EDS spectroscopy was obtained to understand the quantitative elemental makeup of the HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs. The spectra showed the presence of Zn, C and O elements in ample quantity corelating the elemental composition of the framework. On the other hand, the qualitative elemental mapping also confirms the above result (Fig. 10C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and drug release studies
ce:section.ce:para: The drug loading efficiency of ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs was performed using anticancer drug; 5-FU. The drug entrapment efficiency and loading efficiency was found to be 48.2% and 23% respectively. To simulate the intracellular and extracellular environment, two different buffers with pH 5.5 and 7.4 were respectively used in examining the drug release studies. The tumour cells exhibit acidic pH owing to the hypoxic conditions and thus it is important to know the drug release pattern of the prepared nanoformulation in such environment [12]. The results obtained ( Fig. 11A) indicated the more than 90% of the drug was released after 24 h indicating the pH-responsive behaviour the HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs. In addition, the release of drug from HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs in pH 7.4 buffer was less than 20% at the end of 24 h. These results were in similar agreement with our previous reports [12,13] and suggest that the drug loaded framework disrupts in the acidic pH (5.5) and remains intact in the physiological pH (7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and cellular uptake studies
ce:section.ce:para: The hyaluronic acid modified ZIF-8 has been studies in detail by our group and we have demonstrated insignificant toxicity of HA coated ZIF-8 both in vitro as well as in vivo in animals. Similar to previously reported results, in present case more than 80% cells were found to be viable even at higher concentration of 20 µg/mL of HA modified ZIF-8 confined C-Dots. The cellular uptake studies demonstrated efficient internalization of nanoparticles inside IMR-32 cells (Fig. 11 D) which can be attributed to presence of hyaluronic acid which facilitates uptake of nanoparticles via CD-44 mediated uptake.

Protein corona formation plays a major role in the determining the in-vivo stability of NPs [36]. Hence, the interaction of the nanoparticles with BSA protein was studied by assessing the particle size and zeta potential (Fig. 11B). HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot had particle size of 135.2 nm and 142.5 nm and a zeta potential of 22.3 mV and 18 mV respectively at the end of 4 h for the samples incubated in 2% and 4% BSA. The surface modification of the NPs with HA helps in reducing the positive charge of ZIF-8 surface which in turn helps in minimizing the interaction of NPs with serum proteins. These results confirm the stability of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs in the biological media.

It is vital to examine the interaction of the NPs with erythrocyte before administering specially when the desired route of administration is intravenous injection. RBCs play a role of osmometers and any changes in blood may lead to the lysis of RBCs which results in release of the haemoglobin followed by its excretion in the urine (diagnosis parameter of blood poisoning) [37]. Hence, we examined the interaction of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot with RBCs via haemolysis assay and microscopic studies using Motic microscope. The microscopic images of the RBCs incubated with HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot (Fig. 11C. B) were similar to those incubated with saline (Fig. 11C. A) and exhibited no change in the morphology symbolizing safety of the NPs. The haemolysis assay showed that the %haemolysis induced by the HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot (5 mg/mL), was less than 5% (critical safe value) [17] of haemolysis. These studies signify the compatibility and biosafety of HA@ZIF-8 @C-dot NPs.

In this study, C-dots were synthesized using a modified microwave method, and their structural and optical properties were characterized. The C-dots were then loaded onto the ZIF-8 and surface modified with hyaluronic acid using carbodiimide chemistry. The particle properties and surface morphology of the synthesized nanocomposite was characterized using zetasizer and electronic microscopy. Structural characterization was performed using FTIR spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and XRD. The C-dots exhibited a hexagonal graphite structure with functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface, as confirmed by FTIR and XPS spectra. The optical properties of the C-dots showed absorption in the UV region and green emission under 405 nm excitation. The concentration-dependent emission study revealed that the increase in photoluminescence intensity with the increase in the concentration of C-dots up to 0.25 mg/mL. The zeta potential of the C-dots in DI water was − 52.5 mV, indicating their strong electrostatic stabilization in aqueous media. The pH-dependent emission study demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots increased gradually from pH 3–9 and then decreased rapidly at higher pH values. The drug release study showed that the 5-FU-loaded ZIF-8 @C-dots exhibited sustained release of the drug over time. Cell viability and cellular uptake studies using neuroblastoma cells confirmed the biocompatibility and cellular internalization of the HA-modified nanoparticles. The developed nanocomposite exhibited good drug loading capacity and pH responsive drug release property showing its therapeutic ability. The interaction studies confirmed the biocompatibility of the nanocomposite with erythrocytes. These findings provide encouraging results for the further development and investigation of the hybrid nanocomposites. Prognostic behaviour of such nanocomposites against cancer as well as in the biosensing of numerous enzymes will be the subject of our upcoming research.

Shivanand H. Nannuri: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Software. Abhijeet Pandey: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Visualization. Sanjay Kulkarni: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Prashant Deshmukh: Writing – review & editing. Sajan George: Resources, Supervision. Srinivas Mutalik: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are also thankful to Manipal College of Pharmaceutical Sciences and Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, India, for providing the necessary facilities to carry out this research.
